based_on: Two XOR-sigmoid 2-2-1 first-step receipts produced by src/general-engine.ts. Step 0's input is the canonical XOR_INPUT (operator-chosen init w_x1_h1=0.1, etc. — same as fixtures/xor.golden.jsonl). Step 1's parameters_before correctly chains from step 0's parameters_after (Rule 9 stays clean), but step 1's trace_id differs from step 0's so Rule 10 fires.

mutation.rationale: Two visually-distinct lowercase-hex 128-bit identifiers. The 'aaaa...' / 'bbbb...' pattern is unambiguous — any human reading the bad fixture sees the mismatch immediately. The schema requires trace_id to match /^[0-9a-f]{32}$/; both values satisfy the pattern, so the per-record schema validation PASSES on both steps. The failure is a CROSS-RECORD invariant only detectable when the verifier reads both records together.
what_was_NOT_mutated: step 1's parameters_before (correctly chains from step 0's parameters_after — Rule 9 PASSES)
step 1's forward / loss / backward / updates / parameters_after / post_update_forward / post_update_loss — all engine-computed and internally consistent (Rules 1-8 PASS standalone)

single_target_invariant: Reconciliation produces exactly one Rule 10 failure naming both trace_ids in the mismatch. Rule 9 (parameter chain) MUST NOT fire — step 1's parameters_before correctly chains from step 0's parameters_after. Per-step Rules 1-8 MUST pass on both step 0 AND step 1 (each receipt is internally consistent on its own — the mutation only breaks the cross-step trace identity).
rule_10_specification: For multi-record JSONL containing N >= 2 receipts, ALL receipts MUST share the same trace_id AND step_index MUST be sequential (0, 1, 2, ..., N-1 — monotonic and dense; no gaps, no duplicates, no out-of-order indices). Rule 10 covers two distinct failure modes: trace_id mismatch (this fixture) and step_index gap / duplicate / out-of-order. Single-record JSONL skips Rule 10 entirely (there is no cross-record invariant to check). The trace_id pattern follows W3C TraceContext: 128-bit lowercase hex (32 characters).
alternative_rule_10_failure_modes: Future bad-fixture work could cover the OTHER Rule 10 violation paths: (a) step_index gap (records have step_index 0, 1, 3 — missing 2); (b) step_index duplicate (records have 0, 1, 1); (c) step_index out-of-order (records have 0, 2, 1); (d) step_index reset (records have 0, 1, 0); (e) missing step_index on one record but present on another (illegal because schema's allOf requires both or neither). v0.3.0 ships only the trace_id-mismatch case as the minimum gate; the others are deferred to v0.3.x.
purpose: Anti-cross-run-concatenation gate. Without Rule 10, a malicious actor could concatenate single-step receipts from independent training runs and pass them off as a multi-step trace — defeating the entire Proof-of-Learning chain-integrity claim. The trace_id field is the load-bearing W3C-TraceContext-inspired identifier that binds receipts into a single training run. Written BEFORE the multi-record verifier so the verifier cannot be co-developed against engine output.
v0_3_extensions: v0.3.x may add: a 3+ step bad fixture mixing two different trace_ids in arbitrary positions; a fixture with valid trace_ids but invalid step_index sequence; a fixture combining Rule 9 + Rule 10 failures (both fire — verifier should report both, not just one).
error_message_format_note: Rule 10 failure messages should follow the existing renderFailure format from src/bin/bp.ts but include BOTH offending trace_id values so the operator can immediately see which receipts to investigate. Suggested form: 'Rule 10: trace_id mismatch — record 0 trace_id=<aaaa...>, record 1 trace_id=<bbbb...>'. Step-index violations follow analogous shape: 'Rule 10: step_index gap — record 0 step_index=0, record 1 step_index=3 (expected 1)'.
